heb.paragraph: "My guilt (ʿāwōn) has overwhelmed me like a burden too heavy to bear" (v.4). The third penitential psalm. The most physically graphic of the penitential psalms: wounds fester (v.5), back filled with searing pain (v.7), body numb (v.8), heart pounds, strength fails, sight dims (v.10). Friends and companions avoid him (v.11). Sin’s effects are total: body, soul, relationships.

refs.note: Both are penitential psalms opening with 'LORD, do not rebuke me in your anger.' Psalm 6 is shorter but covers the same ground: physical suffering, tears, and the plea for mercy over judgment.
Job's complaint — 'the arrows of the Almighty are in me' — echoes v. 2: 'your arrows have pierced me.' Both texts use the divine-archer metaphor for suffering experienced as God's direct action.
The Servant 'carried our sorrows' and was 'stricken by God.' The psalmist's experience of sin-induced suffering anticipates the Servant who voluntarily bears others' punishment.

notes.note: MacArthur: David acknowledges that his suffering flows from his own sin, not unjust persecution. This distinguishes Psalm 38 from imprecatory psalms — here the discipline is deserved. God's chastening is an expression of his love (Heb 12:6).
MacArthur: even when words fail, God knows the believer's heart. 'All my longings lie open before you' is the foundation of prayer — not eloquence but transparency before an all-knowing God.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the opening echoes Psalm 6:1 almost verbatim — a formulaic plea against divine wrath. But where Psalm 6 is six verses, Psalm 38 extends the suffering to twenty-two (the number of Hebrew letters), making the agony comprehensive.

Alter: the body catalogue — no health, no soundness, wounds festering, guilt overwhelming — is the most graphic physical description of suffering in the Psalter. The body becomes a map of sin's consequences.

Calvin: the psalmist does not deny deserving rebuke — he asks only that it not come in anger. Calvin distinguishes between God's corrective discipline (which the believer accepts) and his punitive wrath (which the believer dreads).
Calvin: 'my guilt has overwhelmed me like a burden too heavy to bear' — Calvin: the consciousness of sin is itself a form of punishment. Before any external consequence, guilt crushes from within.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: this is one of seven traditional penitential psalms (6, 32, 38, 51, 102, 130, 143). The superscription 'for remembrance' (lehazkîr) may link it to the memorial offering (Lev 2:2), a liturgical association.

NET: 'your arrows have pierced me' — divine arrows as instruments of disease or punishment appear in ancient Near Eastern literature. The image conveys that the suffering is perceived as coming directly from God, not merely from circumstances.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: the psalmist describes three dimensions of suffering simultaneously: physical illness (no health, festering wounds), spiritual guilt (iniquity, sinful folly), and social isolation (friends and companions avoid). The suffering is total.

Kidner: 'All my longings lie open before you' — in the midst of graphic suffering, this quiet verse is the psalm's anchor. God already knows. The prayer does not inform God but expresses trust that he sees.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the opening plea against wrath (cf. Ps 6:1) reveals the psalmist's theology of suffering: his affliction comes from God's hand. This is not fatalism but covenant theology — the God who disciplines is the God who heals.

VanGemeren: 'Lord, all my desire is before you' — the Hebrew ta'awāh ('desire, longing') expresses the deepest orientation of the soul. Even in suffering, the psalmist's fundamental desire is directed toward God.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: 'your arrows have pierced me' — the arrow metaphor makes God the attacker. The psalmist does not soften this: the suffering comes from God. Honesty about the source of pain is itself an act of faith.

Goldingay: the physical symptoms — festering wounds, burning back, feebleness — may be literal illness or metaphorical descriptions of guilt's physical effects. The psalms rarely distinguish between the two, because in Hebrew thought body and soul are unified.

heb.paragraph: "LORD, I wait (qiwwîṭî) for you; you will answer, Lord my God" (v.15). The patience of faith in physical extremity. David confesses his sin (v.18), acknowledges his guilt (v.18), and asks God not to forsake him (v.21). "Come quickly to help me, my Lord and my Saviour" (v.22).

refs.note: The plea 'do not be far from me, LORD' (v. 21) echoes Psalm 22:11. Both psalms climax with urgent requests for divine proximity when all human support has vanished.
The combination of sin-confession and hope — 'I wait for you, LORD; I confess my iniquity' — mirrors the De Profundis pattern: repentance is the ground of hope, not its obstacle.

notes.note: MacArthur: in the midst of overwhelming suffering, David anchors his hope in one certainty: 'you will answer, Lord my God.' This models the believer's response to discipline — continued trust in God's faithfulness even when relief is delayed.

MacArthur: the psalm's final cry — 'come quickly to help me' — echoes the urgency of believers throughout Scripture who wait for God's deliverance. The Lord does not always act on our timeline, but he is never indifferent.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: 'I am like the deaf, who cannot hear, like the mute, who cannot speak' — the psalmist chooses silence not from indifference but from helplessness. He has no defence to offer, no argument to make. He is mute before God and man.

Alter: 'Lord, do not forsake me…come quickly to help me, my Lord and my Savior' — the psalm's final words are pure plea. No resolution, no thanksgiving, no confidence formula. It ends in the dark, reaching toward God.

Calvin: the chosen silence is not stoicism but submission. Calvin: when we have sinned, self-defence is dishonest and self-accusation is redundant (God already knows). The only honest posture is silence before the judge.

Calvin: the psalm ends without resolution — a rare and honest conclusion. Calvin notes that not every prayer receives an immediate answer. The psalm models faithfulness in the dark: continuing to call on God when nothing has changed.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: 'I wait for you, Lord; you will answer, Lord my God' — this is the psalm's only expression of confidence. It stands alone between the suffering descriptions, a single pillar of faith in a collapsing structure.
NET: 'my Lord and my Savior' — 'adōnāy tešû'ātî. The possessive 'my Savior' (literally 'my salvation') personalises the divine rescue. Salvation is not abstract theology but personal relationship.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: the single verse of trust (v.15) is surrounded by suffering on every side. Kidner: this is what faith looks like in extremity — not a chapter of theology but a single sentence held against the darkness.
Kidner: the psalm does not resolve. It ends with a cry, not a conclusion. Kidner: sometimes the most faithful thing a psalm can do is refuse to pretend that everything is already fine.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: 'you will answer' — the psalmist's confidence is in God's character, not in visible evidence. Faith here is anticipatory: it claims the answer before seeing it. This is the essence of Hebrews 11:1.

VanGemeren: the psalm's unresolved ending is theologically significant. Not all biblical prayers end in praise. The canon includes darkness, waiting, and unanswered pleading — validating the believer's experience of divine silence.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: the self-imposed silence recalls the suffering servant of Isaiah 53:7. The psalmist, like the servant, does not open his mouth. Whether this is resignation or discipline, it acknowledges that human words are insufficient.

Goldingay: the psalm's refusal to resolve is its most powerful theological statement. Life does not always move from lament to praise within a single liturgy. Some nights do not end in morning — at least not yet.

ppl.name: David
ppl.role: Sufferer acknowledging sin as the root of his agony (vv.1-8,17-22)
ppl.text: This is the third of the seven Penitential Psalms (6, 32, 38, 51, 102, 130, 143). David's suffering is comprehensive: arrows stuck in him (v.2), festering wounds from his 'sinful folly' (v.5), a bent and bowed body (v.6), burning loins (v.7), and a pounding heart (v.10). Unlike Job who protests innocence, David explicitly connects his suffering to sin: 'my guilt has overwhelmed me like a burden too heavy to bear' (v.4). Yet the psalm ends not with resolution but with raw dependence: 'Come quickly to help me, my Lord and my Savior' (v.22).

ppl.name: God (Yahweh)
ppl.role: The one whose rebuke David endures yet trusts (vv.1-2,15,21-22)
ppl.text: David pleads 'do not rebuke me in your anger or discipline me in your wrath' (v.1) — not asking to avoid consequences entirely but for mercy within judgment. Despite feeling God's hand 'heavy upon me' (v.2), David still addresses him as 'LORD, I wait for you; you will answer, Lord my God' (v.15). The tension — suffering from God yet trusting in God — is the psalm's theological heart.

ppl.name: Friends and Companions
ppl.role: Those who abandon David in his suffering (vv.11-12)
ppl.text: David's loved ones 'stand at a distance' and his neighbors 'stay far away' (v.11) — social abandonment compounding physical pain. Meanwhile enemies 'lay snares' and 'speak of ruin all day long' (v.12). This isolation of the sufferer prefigures Christ's passion, where friends fled and enemies closed in. The psalm captures a universal experience: illness and guilt together repel the very community the sufferer needs most.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVMy guilt has overwhelmed me like a burden too heavy to bear.ESVFor my iniquities have gone over my head; like a heavy burden, they are too heavy for me.NIVMy back is filled with searing pain; there is no health in my body.ESVFor my sides are filled with burning, and there is no soundness in my flesh.

rows.label: Physical Agony — 38:1–12
rows.text: Arrows, festering wounds, numbness, dim sight. Sin’s total devastation on body and relationships.

hebtext.note: "My guilt (ʿāwōn) has overwhelmed me like a burden too heavy to bear" (v.4). The third penitential psalm. The most physically graphic of the penitential psalms: wounds fester (v.5), back filled with searing pain (v.7), body numb (v.8), heart pounds, strength fails, sight dims (v.10). Friends and companions avoid him (v.11). Sin’s effects are total: body, soul, relationships.

"LORD, I wait (qiwwîṭî) for you; you will answer, Lord my God" (v.15). The patience of faith in physical extremity. David confesses his sin (v.18), acknowledges his guilt (v.18), and asks God not to forsake him (v.21). "Come quickly to help me, my Lord and my Saviour" (v.22).

themes.note: Judgment: sin’s physical consequences are detailed. Faith: waiting for God in extreme pain. Holiness: sin’s devastation reveals its weight.
